title: Module E: Operating system security
summary: A from-zero tour of how an operating system works (von Neumann hardware, kernel vs user mode, processes, system calls and their types) and how it enforces security: protection rings, memory protection, access control models (DAC/MAC/RBAC), protection systems and labels, file permissions, ACLs and the access control matrix, the reference monitor (interface, authorization module, policy store), security kernels, and real-world examples from UNIX, Windows, and Android.
objectives: Explain what an operating system does and why it sits between users and hardware
Describe the von Neumann architecture and how software moves from storage into RAM to execute
Distinguish kernel mode from user mode and describe how system calls cross that boundary
Classify the main system-call types (process control, file management, device management, protection)
Describe protection rings, memory address spaces, and how the OS isolates processes
Compare DAC, MAC, and RBAC, and read UNIX-style file permissions
Explain protection systems, mandatory protection state, labeling and transition states
Distinguish ACLs from capabilities and read an access control matrix
Explain the reference monitor (interface, authorization module, policy store) and its three guarantees (complete mediation, tamperproof, verifiable)
Describe the UNIX, Windows, and Android security models including SELinux, sandboxing and verified boot

blocks.text: 1. What is an operating system?

An **operating system (OS)** is an organized collection of software that extends the hardware. The professor frames it three ways:

- a set of **control routines** for operating the computer (e.g., gaining access to resources like file I/O)
- an **environment for executing programs**
- an **interface between the end user and the hardware**

Without an OS, every program would have to talk to the disk, RAM, and CPU directly. The OS hides those hardware details so developers and users do not have to deal with them.

**The hardware underneath: the von Neumann architecture.** Modern general-purpose computers follow the **von Neumann model**, where a single **main memory** holds *both* the program instructions and the data they operate on, and the **CPU** repeatedly fetches an instruction from memory and executes it. Because instructions and data share the same memory, software can be loaded, replaced, and run dynamically — which is exactly what an OS exploits when it loads applications into RAM.

**How software actually runs.** Instructions (software) live in persistent storage (HDD, SSD). To execute, they must be copied into **main memory (RAM)**, because the CPU fetches and executes instructions from RAM.

- On a simple single-application device (e.g., an IoT board), a tiny program called the **bootloader** copies the one application into RAM at startup.
- On a general-purpose machine, the **OS replaces the bootloader** for user applications: it loads programs into RAM and allocates memory on request. To do this it runs at a **higher privilege level** than the applications. This privileged loading **hides hardware-specific details** from developers and users.

**Two extremes from the slides:**

- **Arduino** — runs a single task, has **no OS**, and uses a single shared memory space. Simple, but no isolation and no multitasking.
- **FreeBSD** (a UNIX variant) — a full OS with **multi-tasking**, **multi-user** support, and **process lifecycle management**. Many programs and users coexist safely on one machine.

The OS **manages memory**, **manages processes** (services and applications), **manages connected devices** (external and internal), **manages files**, and provides **security**. Everything else builds on these jobs.
2. OS services and the user interface

The OS provides an execution environment through a set of **services**:

- **User interface** — Command-Line Interface (CLI) or Graphical User Interface (GUI)
- **Program execution** — load a program into memory, run it, handle errors
- **I/O operations** — manage input/output with storage devices
- **Filesystem manipulation** — create and manage files and their **permissions**
- **Communications** — let running programs talk to each other
- **Error detection**, **resource allocation** (in a multi-user/multi-program setting where different entities request system resources), and **logging** (keeping track of events that happened on the system)
- **Protection and security**

The slides draw a precise line:

- **Protection** = controlling access to all resources **inside** the system.
- **Security** = handling access attempts from **outsiders** of the system (e.g., **user authentication**).

Protection is the internal rulebook; security is the perimeter.

**The two interfaces.** The **CLI (shell)** is the first, most direct way to send commands. It can live inside the OS core (the **kernel**) or run as a separate system program; it fetches commands (program names or built-ins) and executes them. The **GUI** is friendlier — mouse, keyboard, monitor, icons representing files/programs/actions — and ships with most commercial OSes. Both are just front-ends to the same underlying services.
3. The kernel/user boundary and system calls

The OS runs its most sensitive code (the **kernel**) at a **higher, privileged level**; ordinary applications run at a lower, restricted level. This split is the heart of OS security: a buggy or malicious app should not be able to touch hardware or other programs directly. So how does an app ask the OS to do privileged things like reading a file? Through **system calls**.

A **system call** is the programming interface the OS exposes to let programs access its services. It is typically written in a high-level language (**C, C++, Rust**). Programs rarely invoke it raw; they go through a higher-level **Application Programming Interface (API)**. Standards include **Win32** (Windows), **POSIX** (Linux, macOS, Solaris), and the **Java API** (the JVM).

**How a system call is dispatched (figure in words):**

- Each system call is associated with a **number**.
- The OS keeps a **table** indexed by these numbers.
- The **system-call interface** looks up the number, runs the requested call, and returns the result.
- The caller never needs to know *how* the call is implemented — it only follows the API rules.

This is the boundary crossing: the program triggers a controlled switch from user mode into the kernel, the kernel does the privileged work, and control returns to the program.

The slides group system calls into four families. Knowing the categories helps you reason about *which* sensitive operations the OS mediates:

- **Process control** — creation/termination, loading/execution, getting/setting process attributes, waiting for time or an event, allocating and freeing memory, and debugging.
- **File management** — creation/deletion, opening/closing, reading/writing.
- **Device management** — reading/writing, requesting/releasing a device, getting/setting device attributes.
- **Protection** — access control to resources, getting and setting permissions, allowing and denying user access.

The **protection** category is where access-control decisions are actually exposed to programs.

blocks.title: System-call sequence: copying one file into another
blocks.text:
The slides give a concrete picture of how several system calls chain together to do a familiar task — copying the contents of one file into another. Conceptually the program must:

- **open** the source file for reading (and **open**/**create** the destination file for writing)
- repeatedly **read** a chunk from the source into a buffer, then **write** that chunk to the destination, until end-of-file
- **close** both files when done

Each of these steps (open, read, write, close) is a separate system call from the **file-management** family. A single high-level operation a user thinks of as 'copy a file' is really a *sequence* of mediated calls into the kernel, each one a controlled crossing of the user/kernel boundary.

blocks.title: How printf() becomes a system call (Standard C Library example)

steps.text: The **standard C library** catches this request — in UNIX/Linux it acts as a **bridge** between user applications and the kernel, hiding the complexity of the system-call interface so programs never talk to hardware directly.
The library translates it into the appropriate system-level command, here the **write()** system call.
The **kernel** processes the request and returns a result to the library.
The library relays that return value back to the original program.

blocks.text:
**A real POSIX call.** `ssize_t read(int fd, void *buf, size_t count)` reads from a file:

- `int fd` — which file to read (a file descriptor)
- `void *buf` — buffer where the read data is stored
- `size_t count` — maximum number of bytes to read

A program calls this, but the actual reading from disk happens in the kernel.

blocks.title: Opening a file with permissions: open()
blocks.text:
Creating/opening a file shows how permissions enter at the system-call level. From the slides:

```c
mode_t mode = S_IRUSR | S_IWUSR | S_IRGRP | S_IROTH;
fd = open("/tmp/file", O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, mode);
```

- The **flags** (`O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC`) say *how* to open: write-only, create if missing, truncate to zero length.
- The **mode** bits set the file's **permissions** when it is created: `S_IRUSR` (owner read) + `S_IWUSR` (owner write) + `S_IRGRP` (group read) + `S_IROTH` (others read) — i.e., `rw-r--r--`.

A recurring OS design idea: **policy** is *what* needs to be done; **mechanism** is *how* it is done. The mechanism is **transparent to the policy**. A user states a policy (a set of rules, e.g., security requirements) without wanting to implement it personally; the system is in charge of following those rules and implementing a mechanism to enforce them. The same rule can have **different implementations**.

**Common exam mistake:** confusing policy and mechanism. "Only the owner may read this file" is a **policy** (a rule). The permission-bit check the kernel performs to enforce it is the **mechanism**. They are deliberately separate so the mechanism can change without changing the policy.

question.prompt: An application needs to write data to a file on disk. How does it perform this privileged operation?

options.content: It writes directly to the disk controller hardware in user mode
It issues a system call, which switches into the kernel to do the privileged work and return a result
It must be rewritten to run inside the bootloader
It modifies the system-call table to grant itself disk access

question.explanation: Applications run in restricted user mode and cannot touch hardware directly, so A is wrong. They request services via **system calls**, which cross into the privileged kernel and return a result (B). The bootloader only runs at startup (C). The caller never modifies the system-call table — it just follows the API rules (D).

blocks.text: 4. Protection rings, address spaces, and isolation

A **protection ring** is a security boundary that separates the **kernel** from **user-level processes** — it enforces the privileged/unprivileged split in hardware. UNIX adopted protection rings (an idea inspired by the Multics system, along with encrypted password storage and access control lists).

Every process is given a unique **address space**: the range of memory locations it is allowed to access. Modern UNIX systems implement this by defining which **memory pages** a process may touch. If a process tries to read outside its space, the hardware/OS blocks it — this is **memory protection**, and it is what stops one process from spying on or corrupting another.

**Isolation is the fundamental goal.** Enforcing separation among components in the same system is fundamental; the slides list four ways to do it:

- **Physical isolation** — give each module/process its own dedicated hardware terminal or device
- **Temporal isolation** — run processes at separate times, never concurrently
- **Logical isolation** — the OS gives each user an abstracted, isolated workspace without exposing internal system details (the everyday case)
- **Cryptographic isolation** — use encryption to protect and hide sensitive data from unauthorized access

A cornerstone of cybersecurity: the ultimate aim is to restrict a process strictly to the actions it needs to perform — every **user and process** should operate with **only** the permissions essential to its role, the **minimum access required** to fulfil its goals. Advantages from the slides:

- **System stability** — restricting the range of changes code can make simplifies testing its behavior and interactions.
- **System security** — limiting a program's system-wide actions prevents a vulnerability in one program from compromising the entire system.
- **Simplified deployment** — apps needing fewer permissions are easier to integrate and manage in complex environments.
5. Access control: subjects, objects, and the matrix

In information technology, **security** means protecting digital information and IT systems from threats — whether those threats are **deliberate or accidental**, and whether they come from **inside or outside** the organization. It relies on **identifying risks, blocking potential attacks, and responding to incidents**, using security protocols, dedicated software, and IT support services. In short: security is about defending your valuable **digital assets**.

A mechanism that lets an organization's leadership specify and regulate which **subjects** (active entities — users, applications, services) may interact with which **objects** (passive entities — files, network sockets, databases, processes), and the actions they are authorized to perform. Rights are things like **read, write, execute, list, modify, delete**, and they are enforced using administrative, technical, and physical safeguards. Two foundations: a **security framework** (the rules/policy) and a **reference monitor** (the enforcer).

**Objects that typically need protection.** The slides enumerate the resources an OS must guard:

- system memory
- files stored on disk
- running processes
- file directories
- execution stacks
- critical instructions, especially those with **elevated privileges**
- user credentials and authentication systems

Two objectives work together to secure these: **verify every access attempt** (a prior grant does not mean unlimited or permanent access) and **apply least privilege** (grant only the minimum set of resources needed).

**The access control matrix (ACM)** organizes this as a table:

- each **row** is a subject (e.g., a user)
- each **column** is an object (e.g., a file)
- each **cell** lists the rights that subject has over that object

Reading a single row tells you everything a subject is allowed to do — its **protection domain**. The **protection state** is the snapshot of subjects, objects, and authorized actions, plus the operations that can change it.

**ACLs and capabilities — guarding the matrix.** File operations like editing or deleting are regulated through **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** or **capabilities**:

- An **Access Control List (ACL)** is associated with each object: for **every object there is a single ACL**, which lists every subject permitted to access that object and its level of access. Formally, the ACL holds all the non-empty entries that would appear in the object's **column** of the access control matrix. As the dual of this, the slides note that **a directory is maintained for each subject** — so each object keeps a list of subjects, while each subject keeps a directory of what it can reach.
- **Capabilities** offer **stronger security** and are commonly implemented in operating systems managing file systems like **NTFS** and **ext3**.

In some filesystem designs only specific users may change ACL settings or even detect that a file exists — limiting access reduces risk and protects integrity.
6. Protection systems and mandatory protection

A **protection system** is composed of a **protection state** — which describes the actions that subjects are permitted to perform on objects — plus a collection of **operations** that allow controlled changes to that state. It both establishes and administers the protection state, and the operations enable **dynamic** control over access rights. The **access matrix** is the framework used to outline each process's **protection domain**.

The access-matrix model has a weakness: **untrusted processes can interfere with the protection mechanism**. Through state-changing operations, an untrusted user process can edit the access matrix — adding subjects, objects, or permissions in its cells. For example, if **Process 1 owns File 1, it can grant other processes read, write, or even ownership** over File 1. A protection framework that lets untrusted processes alter the protection state is a **discretionary access control (DAC)** system.

A **mandatory protection system** can be modified **only by authorized administrators, through trusted software**. It is defined by three state representations:

- **Mandatory protection state** — subjects and objects are assigned specific **labels** that determine the actions subjects may perform on objects.
- **Labeling state** — the mapping that links processes and resource objects to their respective labels.
- **Transition state** — the authorized procedures by which processes and objects may **change label**.

The protection status is characterized by **immutable labels**; this fixed labeling, together with defined transitions, controls labels for subjects and objects.
7. DAC, MAC, and RBAC

The **owner** of a resource decides who may access it — "discretionary" because access is granted at the owner's own judgment. This is the model behind **Windows, Linux, macOS, and most UNIX variants**: create a file, set its permissions, and the OS enforces them. Weakness: an untrusted process that owns an object can change the protection state (grant read/write/ownership to others).

The **system**, not individual users, decides access — only authorized administrators can modify policy, through trusted software. It uses **labels**: subjects get a **security authorization** (e.g., confidential, secret, top secret) and objects get a matching **classification**. These labels, linking authorizations and classifications to subjects and objects, govern access; they are **immutable** except via authorized **transitions**. MAC dominates where protecting sensitive data is critical, e.g., **military** systems. **SELinux** brings MAC to Android/Linux.

Permissions are assigned to **roles** (based on competency, authority level, and responsibilities), and users get roles. A role lets users perform a large spectrum of authorized activities, adjusting permissions dynamically to reflect their functions, relationships, and restrictions. Roles can be created, modified, or retired as the organization changes — so you do not have to update each user's rights one by one.

**Don't confuse DAC and MAC.** The deciding question is *who controls access*: in **DAC the resource owner** decides (and can hand out rights at will); in **MAC the system/administrator** decides via fixed labels and the owner cannot override it. "It's mandatory" means mandatory for users, not optional.

question.prompt: On a military system, a file is labeled 'top secret' and a user holds a 'confidential' authorization. The user owns a different file and wants to grant a colleague read access to it, but the system refuses to let any user re-label data. Which access control model is in force?

options.content: DAC, because the user owns a file
RBAC, because the user has an authorization level
MAC, because the system enforces immutable labels and users cannot change them
No access control is in force

question.explanation: The defining clue is that **the system enforces labels and users cannot re-label data** — that is **MAC** (C), typical of military settings. DAC would let the owner grant access at their discretion (A is the trap). An authorization level alone is not RBAC; RBAC is about roles mapping to permissions (B). Access control is clearly present (D).

blocks.text: 8. The reference monitor and a secure OS

The mechanism that **enforces** access control. It has three parts: an **interface** through which access requests arrive, an **authorization module** that evaluates them, and a **policy store** holding the rules. When a security-sensitive action occurs, the interface fires **hooks** that call the authorization module, which queries the policy store and returns an allow/deny decision (also handling labeling decisions and label transitions based on current state).

**The simplified model (request in, decision out).** The slides also picture the reference monitor at its simplest: it **processes incoming requests and delivers a clear decision**. The decision reflects whether the request **complies with the access-control rules** defined by the reference monitor. So however complex the internals (interface, module, store), from the outside it behaves like a single gate: a request goes in, an allow/deny decision comes out.

**Reference monitor interface.** This specifies the exact points at which the protection system interacts with the reference monitor. It guarantees that **every security-sensitive action is authorized** by the access-control mechanism. A **security-sensitive action** is any operation on an object (file, socket, ...) that could breach the security policy if done without authorization — e.g., the OS preventing one user from reading another user's private key unless explicitly permitted.

**Authorization module.** At the heart of the reference monitor, it takes inputs from the interface — the **process identity**, **references to objects**, and the **name of the system call** — and transforms them into a query for the policy store. Its key challenge is the **mapping**: translating the process identity into a **subject label**, object references into **object labels**, and identifying which operations need authorization (one interface may involve multiple operations). The protection system defines the available labels and operations, but the authorization module is responsible for building an effective mapping to generate the correct query.

**Policy store.** A centralized repository managing protection states, labeling states, and transition conditions. It answers the authorization module's queries:

- **Authorization queries** carry `{subject_label, object_label, operation_set}` and yield a simple **yes/no** decision.
- **Labeling queries** carry `{subject_label, resource}`, where the subject's label and certain resource attributes determine the label assigned to the resource (resources can be dynamic, like processes, or static, like files).
- Some systems also query to validate **state transitions**, ensuring secure changes in system status.

blocks.term: Three guarantees of a secure OS
blocks.text:
A secure OS enforces access control per the reference monitor principle, anchored by three guarantees:

- **Complete Mediation** — the system enforces and evaluates *every* security-sensitive operation
- **Tamperproof** — the access-control framework and its security components cannot be modified by untrusted software
- **Verifiable** — the enforcement mechanism is deliberately small enough to be comprehensively analyzed and tested, confirming it reliably meets its security objectives

Designing an OS means building a security framework robust enough that **no future software can bypass these protections**.

The **security kernel** is the combination of hardware and software that realizes the reference monitor concept. It is the core component enforcing security across the whole OS and the critical interface connecting hardware, the OS, and other components. Caveat from the slides: implementing a security kernel can **impact system speed or increase overall file size**. The concept was first realized by a **MITRE prototype in 1974**.

**Remember the trio:** Complete Mediation (check everything), Tamperproof (can't be altered), Verifiable (small enough to prove correct). If a security check can be bypassed even once, **complete mediation** fails — and the whole guarantee collapses.
9. Filesystem security and authentication

A **filesystem** is a structured way to store and manage files and their data for quick retrieval, implemented across devices (HDD, USB flash, CDs, DVDs, ...). Storage hardware is divided into fixed-size units called **sectors/blocks**, which the filesystem organizes into files and folders, plus an **index** to locate each file's position and content.

**Types of filesystem.** Disk-based examples include **FAT** (File Allocation Table), **NTFS**, **HFS** (Hierarchical File System), **ext2**, **ext3**, **ISO9660**, and **UDF**:

- **Windows** mainly uses **FAT variants (FAT12, FAT16, FAT32)** and especially **NTFS**. FAT remains the go-to format for **floppy disks** and is still in use.
- **macOS** relies on **HFS**.
- **Linux** commonly uses **ext2** and **ext3**.
- Optical media: CDs use **ISO9660**, DVDs use **UDF**.

The filesystem maintains **data integrity** mainly by **enforcing access controls** through ACLs or capabilities (capabilities are stronger and common in NTFS/ext3), with **backup and recovery** as a secondary layer of defense.

**UNIX security model (the canonical example).** UNIX is a multiuser OS originally created by **Dennis Ritchie and Ken Thompson at AT&T Bell Labs**, with security mechanisms inspired by **Multics** (encrypted password storage, protection rings, ACLs):

- The kernel plus key privileged processes form the **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)**; these run as **root** (superuser) with **unrestricted access** to all resources. Root processes handle startup, user authentication, system management, and network services.
- Every other process carries a **user identity** that limits its file access.
- In UNIX, **everything is a file** — secondary storage, I/O devices, network interfaces, and IPC channels are all represented as files, so file permissions become a universal control.
- The goal: protect **users from each other** and protect the **TCB from users**.

**Reading UNIX permission bits.** A file shows `rw- r-- r--` for **owner / group / others**:

- owner: **read + write**
- group: **read** only
- others: **read** only

This is **DAC**: the owner set these and the OS enforces them. UNIX uses simple **mode bits**; **Windows** uses richer, more granular **ACLs** and attaches a **token** (encapsulating identity, permissions, and rights) to each process. Authentication (login, password check) is what binds a real person to that identity in the first place — recall: **security** handles outsiders via **user authentication**.
10. Windows and Android security models

**Windows.** Microsoft Windows traces back to **MS-DOS**, the foundational OS launched for IBM PCs in **1981**. The **Windows 2000** security framework **mirrors UNIX's DAC** model — managing protection states, object labels, and transitions between protection domains — but with greater **extensibility** and support for a **broader range of security policies**. That extra flexibility, however, introduces **additional challenges** in maintaining security. Key specifics:

- **Subjects**: as in UNIX, each process is linked to a **token** encapsulating its identity; one identity maps to a single user, but the token can mix many permissions and rights.
- **Objects**: Windows manages many object types beyond files. Software can introduce new object categories and register them in the **Active Directory**, the system's hierarchical namespace for all recognized objects. Each object type is defined by the operations it supports.
- **Permissions**: Windows uses flexible, **granular ACLs**, versus UNIX's simpler **mode bits** system.

**Android (built on a customized Linux kernel + open-source components).** Designed mainly for touchscreen devices, its security model layers several mechanisms:

- **App sandboxing** — each app runs isolated from the rest of the system and can access other resources only if the user **explicitly grants permission** during installation.
- **Per-app UNIX identity** — Android uses a UNIX-like permission system; each app runs under a **unique user identity**, so one app cannot read/modify another's files unless the developer explicitly allows sharing.
- **SELinux** — Security-Enhanced Linux enforces strict access-control policies, implementing **MAC** on running processes.
- **Device Administration API** — since **Android 2.2**, offers system-level device-management capabilities for enhanced control and security.
- **System partition + Safe Mode** — the system partition (kernel, libraries, runtime, framework, built-in apps) is **locked read-only** to protect integrity; in **Safe Mode**, third-party apps do not auto-start and must be launched manually.

**Android, continued — Linux-kernel foundations and verified boot.** Key security capabilities the Linux kernel provides Android:

- a **user-role-based permissions system**
- **process isolation** to prevent unauthorized interactions
- a flexible, secure **inter-process communication (IPC)** mechanism
- the ability to **strip unnecessary/vulnerable kernel components**

The core Linux approach prevents interference between users: user A cannot access user B's files, reduce B's memory allocation, monopolize B's CPU time, or abuse shared hardware (telephony, GPS, Bluetooth) assigned to B.

**Verified Boot.** From **Android 6.0**, devices support verified boot with **device-mapper-verity (dm-verity)**, ensuring software integrity from the **hardware root of trust** through to the system partition — each boot phase cryptographically validates the next stage before running it. From **Android 7.0** onward, verified boot is enforced rigorously, **blocking devices with compromised software from completing boot**.

**Big picture to carry away:** the OS sits between users and hardware and enforces security by (1) splitting privileged kernel code from restricted user code via protection rings, (2) isolating processes in their own address spaces, (3) mediating every security-sensitive action through a reference monitor under a chosen model (DAC/MAC/RBAC), and (4) applying least privilege everywhere. Android shows these working together: a per-app **unique user identity** and **sandbox**, **SELinux** enforcing **MAC**, a **read-only** system partition, and **verified boot** from a hardware root of trust — physical, logical, and cryptographic isolation combined.
